extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The Physical Twin is a thermal incubator composed of an insulated styrofoam box that houses a resistive heat‑bed and an internal fan for air circulation. Temperature is measured by two sensors inside the box and one sensor outside. The plant is actuated by a Raspberry Pi that runs a bang‑bang style controller (with lower‑limit, upper‑limit, heating duration and waiting duration parameters) and communicates via a low‑level driver. The system’s purpose is to achieve and maintain a target temperature inside the box regardless of the content, relying on the fan to ensure a uniform temperature distribution.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides (1) real‑time visualization of the incubator status by capturing controller and driver messages, storing them in a time‑series database and displaying them on a dashboard; (2) on‑demand calibration of the thermal models by automatically executing the calibration routine described in the study; (3) anomaly detection and heatbed state estimation using a Kalman filter applied to Model B together with measured data; (4) what‑if simulations through co‑simulation that replay historical or live PT data to evaluate alternative controller parameters; and (5) a self‑adaptation loop that detects behavioral changes, schedules and runs experiments, re‑calibrates Model B, updates the Kalman filter, performs what‑if simulations and re‑configures the controller accordingly.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The DT implementation relies on the open‑source GitHub repository of the incubator case study, the RabbitMQ message broker for synchronisation and communication, a low‑level driver that abstracts sensor and actuator interfacing, and a Raspberry Pi executing the bang‑bang controller logic. Calibration algorithms update the parameters of the two thermal models (Model A and Model B) derived from the basic heat equation. State estimation is performed with a Kalman filter. Visualization uses a time‑series database and dashboard (as described in the goals). What‑if analysis uses co‑simulation techniques that consume PT data via the same RabbitMQ infrastructure.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The incubator Digital Twin follows a centralized orchestration pattern built around a RabbitMQ message broker. The physical twin (PT) is instrumented with a low‑level driver that reads temperature sensors and publishes measurement messages to RabbitMQ. A cyber component (the controller) subscribes to these messages, executes a bang‑bang control law, and publishes actuation commands (fan and heat‑bed on/off) back to the broker. Additional DT services—calibration algorithms, monitors, anomaly‑detection filters and visualization dashboards—listen to the same message streams, consume data, and optionally publish derived information (e.g., calibrated parameters, Kalman‑filter estimates). All cyber components run on a Raspberry Pi or associated host machines, and coordination is achieved through the shared RabbitMQ topic/queue infrastructure, allowing synchronous or asynchronous processing of data and commands.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration with external systems is realized via the RabbitMQ message broker using the AMQP protocol. The low‑level driver and controller expose their telemetry and control interfaces as RabbitMQ messages, enabling other applications (e.g., dashboards, data‑logging services, or external analytics platforms) to subscribe to or publish on the same topics. No additional enterprise‑level standards or APIs are described; the DT relies exclusively on RabbitMQ for interoperability and data exchange with any external consumer that implements AMQP.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The incubator DT incorporates two analytical temperature prediction models derived from an energy‑balance equation (Q = c·m·ΔT) and a power‑input equation (E_power = V·I·Δt): Model A uses two free parameters and relies on four simplifying assumptions, while Model B uses four free parameters and relaxes the assumption that the heat‑bed does not absorb heat, giving it better fidelity for the heat‑bed’s thermal inertia; model parameters are updated by on‑demand calibration algorithms that compare measured temperature data with model outputs. Temperature data are acquired periodically by three temperature sensors (two inside the insulated box, one outside), read by the low‑level driver, and transmitted as messages over a RabbitMQ message broker to the controller and any DT components that consume the data.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation is provided by a resistive heat‑bed (heating element) and an internal fan that circulates air within the insulated styrofoam box. Control is performed by a Raspberry Pi‑based controller executing a bang‑bang control strategy with configurable limits (LL, UL) and timing parameters (heating duration H, waiting duration C). Actuation commands are sent as messages over RabbitMQ; the low‑level driver receives these commands, enforces a built‑in safety protocol, and forwards them to the hardware. The controller can remotely turn the heat‑bed on/off and set fan speed, with safety constraints enforced by the driver’s safe‑state logic.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Three temperature sensors constitute the sensing layer: two sensors monitor the internal air temperature of the styrofoam‑insulated box and one sensor measures the ambient temperature outside the box. The low‑level driver reads these sensors at a periodic (unspecified) interval, packages the raw temperature values into messages, and uploads them via the RabbitMQ server for consumption by the DT; no explicit sampling frequency, resolution, or timestamping details are provided in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is governed by four explicit assumptions: (1) uniform temperature distribution inside the box ensured by the fan, (2) negligible heat accumulation in box walls, (3) constant specific heat capacity of the enclosed air, and (4) the majority of electrical power is converted to heat for the air, ignoring heat absorbed by the heat‑bed and heat generated by the fan. Validity is evaluated by comparing Model A and Model B against experimental temperature traces, with Model B shown to more accurately capture the heat‑bed’s thermal inertia. Calibration procedures serve to quantify and reduce uncertainty in model parameters using the measured temperature data, thereby addressing the uncertainties introduced by the simplifying assumptions.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Physical‑to‑virtual interaction is realised by the low‑level driver that periodically reads the three temperature sensors (two inside the incubator box and one measuring ambient temperature) and publishes the measured temperature values to the RabbitMQ communication server. The driver incorporates a safe protocol to keep the device in a safe state before uploading the data. DT components listen to the RabbitMQ messages for further processing.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Virtual‑to‑physical interaction is performed by the controller running on the Raspberry Pi, which subscribes to command messages from the RabbitMQ communication server and forwards actuation commands for the fan and the heatbed to the low‑level driver. The controller also publishes its current control state back to RabbitMQ, enabling the physical twin to be driven according to the digital twin’s decisions.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The technical connection between the physical and cyber elements is based on a RabbitMQ message broker. RabbitMQ serves as the communication backbone; the low‑level driver, the controller and other DT modules connect to it using RabbitMQ’s standard messaging interface. The broker is described as mature, lightweight and easy to deploy on premises or in the cloud.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The documents state that the RabbitMQ communication server may be deployed either on‑premises or in a cloud environment, and that Digital Twin components (e.g., visualization dashboard, calibration engine) run on machines that can connect to this broker. No explicit details are given regarding computational or storage capacities, scalability mechanisms, deployment models (such as containers or virtual machines), or availability guarantees beyond the broker’s own capabilities.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The incubator Digital Twin delivers decision support through several tightly coupled capabilities. Real‑time visualisation is achieved by streaming controller and low‑level driver messages into a time‑series database and rendering them on an interactive dashboard. On‑demand calibration automatically invokes the calibration routine (section 3.3) to adjust Model B parameters using the latest sensor data. Anomaly detection and heat‑bed state estimation are performed by a Kalman filter that fuses temperature measurements with Model B predictions to estimate the unmeasured heat‑bed temperature and flag deviations from expected behaviour. What‑if simulations are executed via co‑simulation that replays historical or live PT data to evaluate alternative controller settings and optimise parameters. A self‑adaptation loop closes the loop: when the Kalman filter detects a behavioural change (e.g., insertion of a cold object), the DT schedules an experiment, re‑configures the controller for data collection, re‑calibrates Model B with the new data, updates the Kalman filter, runs predictive simulations, and finally re‑programs the controller with the optimised configuration. These functions collectively provide insight generation, predictive analytics, automated decision making, and confidence‑based reconfiguration of the physical twin.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The incubator DT incorporates several safety mechanisms documented in the case study. The low‑level driver embeds a 'safe protocol' that enforces a safe state for the physical twin when abnormal conditions are detected. The DT includes runtime monitors that continuously evaluate sensor data against configured lower‑limit (LL) and upper‑limit (UL) temperature thresholds, flagging violations for human or automated intervention. Anomaly detection is performed by a Kalman‑filter‑based estimator that fuses measured sensor data with Model B to estimate the unmeasured heat‑bed temperature and to identify deviations from expected behavior. Calibration algorithms update model parameters in real time, and the DT framework can trigger reconfiguration of the controller when safety limits are breached. Communication between components uses RabbitMQ as a message broker, but no specific authentication or encryption mechanisms are described.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

This constitutes our future work. Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables **Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series database, and plot the data into a dashboard. **On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented in [15]. **Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature. **What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8. **Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT. We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary** We described the implementation and modelling of the incubator case study. Hardware, software, and datasets are available online [6] . Using this example to set the terminology, we proceeded to demystify the DT concept and its goals. 6 [https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator) 16

For the case study, the RabbitMQ [4] server was deployed for synchronisation and communication. With tens of thousands of users, RabbitMQ is one of the most popular

We choose RabbitMQ because it greatly facilitates the digital twinning process, as
the DT can be implemented by components that listen to the messages being passed

**2.2.2** **The Low-Level Driver**


The low-level driver is used to interact with the PT and to issue the states of the PT

The low level driver periodically reads the temperature data, and checks if there are
commands from the controller, through the communication server. The temperature

The controller used in the case study is a comparable to a bang-bang controller, which
a small difference described below. The control strategy is executed after receiving the

temperature measurements.
The principle of bang-bang control is that when a reference temperature is given
to the controller, the controller issues a heating signal turning on the heatbed until the
measured temperature from sensors reaches the reference temperature. Because of the
delayed effect of the temperature, the controller needs to wait after each actuation, to
make sure the temperature does not rise too much. Figure 8 shows the state chart of the

road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.

PT and its environment [1] ; and

more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).

detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,

**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.
